skill.name: gcp-cloud-run
skill.description: Guides teams through production-oriented Google Cloud Run services, Cloud Run Functions, event triggers, and cold start optimization patterns.
skill.summary: Build and tune serverless applications on Google Cloud Run.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static critical heuristics are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences and deployment examples, not executable skill code. The skill is publishable, but it includes commands that can deploy public Cloud Run services and mutate GCP resources if followed without review.

medium_findings.title: Public Cloud Run Deployment Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples deploy services and functions with unauthenticated access. This is legitimate for public APIs, but it can expose workloads if copied without IAM review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deployment snippets explicitly include unauthenticated access flags. The context is instructional, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Cloud Resource Mutation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Docker, Cloud Build, and gcloud deployment commands. These commands can create, update, or expose GCP resources when executed by an agent or user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible deployment examples and not hidden execution logic. They still carry real cloud-change risk if run without explicit approval.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick findings are false positives from Markdown code fences around Dockerfile, JavaScript, YAML, and bash examples. No Ruby code or automatic shell execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows Markdown fences and example snippets. There is no executable Ruby source file or runtime hook in the scanned content.

low_findings.title: Safe Environment Variable Example
low_findings.description: The environment access finding references reading PORT for a Cloud Run web server. This is a standard platform configuration value, not secret harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code reads only process.env.PORT with a default value. No credential variable names or exfiltration path were found near this example.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Pub/Sub Base64 Decoding
low_findings.description: The base64 decoding example parses a Pub/Sub message payload. This is expected for Google Cloud event processing and does not indicate obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code labels the example as Pub/Sub processing. Base64 decoding is part of normal Pub/Sub message handling.

low_findings.title: Network And Weak Crypto Static Hits Dismissed
low_findings.description: The network hits occur in prose that mentions requests, and the weak cryptographic algorithm hit has no supporting cryptographic code. No credential exfiltration or crypto usage was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are description or anti-pattern prose, not import statements or network calls. I did not find evidence of weak crypto implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unauthenticated Service Exposure Flag
dangerous_patterns.description: Deployment examples use unauthenticated access flags. Users should confirm public exposure is intended before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flags are present in clear deployment examples. The skill does not hide them, but they can create publicly reachable services.

content.user_title: Deploy Cloud Run Services on GCP
content.value_statement: Cloud Run deployments require choices about containers, events, scaling, and startup latency. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for production serverless services on GCP.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Cloud Run services for containerized web APIs.
Shows Cloud Run Functions patterns for HTTP, Pub/Sub, and Cloud Storage events.
Provides Cloud Build and gcloud deployment examples for common workloads.
Recommends cold start improvements such as CPU boost, minimum instances, and smaller images.
Calls out Cloud Run operational risks around concurrency, memory, and background work.
limitations: It does not authenticate to GCP or deploy resources by itself.
It does not replace project-specific IAM, networking, or cost review.
Some snippets are illustrative and need service names, regions, and resource values changed.
It focuses on Node.js examples more than other runtimes.

use_cases.title: Launch a Container API
use_cases.description: Plan a Cloud Run service with a Dockerfile, health endpoint, and Cloud Build deployment flow.

use_cases.title: Process Event Workloads
use_cases.description: Design Cloud Run Functions for HTTP, Pub/Sub, or Cloud Storage triggers with clear handler structure.

use_cases.title: Reduce Startup Latency
use_cases.description: Review cold start causes and choose settings for CPU boost, minimum instances, image size, and lazy loading.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Cloud Run Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me decide between a Cloud Run service and Cloud Run Functions for this workload: [describe workload]. Include tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Deployment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a Cloud Run deployment plan for a Node.js API. Include container, build, region, scaling, and IAM review steps.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Cold Starts
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Cloud Run service design for cold start risks: [paste design]. Recommend practical changes and explain cost impact.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this Cloud Run architecture for production readiness: [paste architecture]. Cover concurrency, timeouts, memory, events, security, and rollback.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy a public Node.js API on Cloud Run.
output: A short recommendation to use a Cloud Run service for a containerized API.
A checklist covering Docker image build, health checks, Cloud Build deployment, scaling, and IAM exposure review.
A warning to confirm public access before using unauthenticated deployment settings.

output_examples.input: My Cloud Run service has slow first requests.
output: A prioritized cold start review covering image size, startup CPU boost, minimum instances, lazy initialization, and memory allocation.
A brief explanation of latency and cost tradeoffs for each change.

output_examples.input: I need to process Pub/Sub messages in GCP.
output: A recommendation to use a Cloud Run Function for simple event processing.
Guidance on message decoding, handler structure, region selection, and operational monitoring.

best_practices: Review IAM and public access before running deployment commands.
Set explicit limits for memory, CPU, timeouts, concurrency, and maximum instances.
Test cold start behavior with realistic dependencies and traffic patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not copy sample service names, regions, or public access flags into production without review.
Do not run long background tasks in request-driven Cloud Run services.
Do not write large temporary files without counting them against memory limits.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must approve and run any GCP commands.

faq.question: Is this skill only for Node.js?
faq.answer: No, but the examples focus on Node.js. The Cloud Run patterns apply to other container runtimes.

faq.question: Can it help with Cloud Run Functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes HTTP, Pub/Sub, and Cloud Storage function patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover cold starts?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests CPU boost, minimum instances, image optimization, lazy loading, and memory tuning.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The examples include deployment commands and public access flags. These need review before use in real projects.

faq.question: Does it include full production security design?
faq.answer: No. It gives practical reminders, but IAM, networking, secrets, and compliance need project-specific review.
